Low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska combined with strong high pressure over interior Alaska produced strong gap winds through the Chugach Mountains along with snow. The deep cold Arctic air also resulted in dangerously low wind chills in the Alaska Range passes in the northern Copper River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska DOT Thompson Pass crew reported blizzard conditions through the region of the Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over interior Alaska combined with low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska resulted in the typical strong outflow gap winds through the Chugach Mountains. The peak wind was around 74 mph in Valdez and likely was gusting to 80 mph out of the Copper River Delta. Strong wind funneled out of Portage Pass into Whittier as wind was measured to 70 mph.

Strong high pressure over interior Alaska combined with low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska resulted in the typical strong outflow gap winds through the Chugach Mountains. The peak wind was around 74 mph in Valdez and likely was gusting to 80 mph out of the Copper River Delta. Strong wind funneled out of Portage Pass into Whittier as wind was measured to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust measured in Valdez and surface analysis conclude the wind gust was likely to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over interior Alaska combined with low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska resulted in the typical strong outflow gap winds through the Chugach Mountains. The peak wind was around 74 mph in Valdez and likely was gusting to 80 mph out of the Copper River Delta. Strong wind funneled out of Portage Pass into Whittier as wind was measured to 70 mph.
A large intense Bering Sea storm produced strong wind across the Aleutian Islands to the Pribilof Islands. The strongest measured wind was 70 KT at Adak.
A large intense Bering Sea storm produced strong wind across the Aleutian Islands to the Pribilof Islands. The strongest measured wind was 70 KT at Adak.
A large intense Bering Sea storm produced strong wind across the Aleutian Islands to the Pribilof Islands. The strongest measured wind was 70 KT at Adak.

An intense and large storm in the Bering Sea produced a long fetch of strong wind across the Bering Sea aligned with the Kuskokwim Delta coast November 6th through the 9th. This produced a surge of up to 5 feet along the Kuskokwim Delta Coast.

A 989 mb low south of Attu the afternoon of February 28 moved eastward and rapidly deepened 25 mb overnight to 964 mb the morning of March 1. It then began to slow its forward movement and slowly weaken as it cut a path just south of the Aleutian Islands through the evening of March 2nd. This storm brought Blizzard conditions to parts of the Aleutians and northward to the Pribilof Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: When the occluded front approached the Dutch Harbor area during the late afternoon to early evening of March 1st the winds rapidly increased and visibility decreased in the snow and blowing snow. The visibility in snow and blowing snow ranged between 1/4 and 1/2 mile for 3 1/2 hours with winds gusting between 69 and 84 mph during that period. The peak of the event was around 6 PM when the peak Southeast wind gust was measured at 84 mph. The visibility was 1/4 mile at this time due to snow and blowing snow. Neither found or received any reports of damage or injuries.

CZ_NAME: CAPE DECISION TO SALISBURY SOUND COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Little Port Walter COOP observer measured 8 inches of new snowfall in 12 hours on March 12th. This was due to training snow showers impacting the SE end of Baranof Island that day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Little Port Walter COOP observer measured 8 inches of new snow from midnight to noon on March 12th. This was due to continuous heavy snow showers moving onto the the SE end of Baranof Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 977 mb low moved north over the Bering Sea on the 5th of March and gradually weakened by early on the 6th north of the Bering Strait. This system brought blizzard conditions to Wales, and near-blizzard conditions to Gambell, Savoonga, and Teller. The Wales AWOS reported a peak gust to 54 kts (63 mph) during the event.

Strong winds developed along the west coast of Alaska between a 1045 high over the Arctic and 977 mb low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska. A gust of 58 kt (67 mph) was recorded at the Wales AWOS during the morning of the 5th.

A strong high pressure center over the far north Arctic Ocean was stationary while a weather front moved northwest over the Chukchi Sea bringing blizzard conditions on the 3rd to Point Lay, Wainwright, and Atqasuk. Visibility at these locations was reduced to one-quarter mile or less in blowing snow.

A 974 MB storm force low moved into the central Gulf of Alaska on the afternoon of Dec 13 with lots of warm moist air moving over the arctic front which was located near Berners Bay. By the afternoon of the 14th a secondary low moved into the eastern Gulf making landfall near Cape Fairweather then redeveloped over the Yukon overnight. The flow regime became southwest on the 15th with some cooler air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downtown Haines got 14 inches of new snow from this storm. Haines Customs got 11.5 inches of new snow storm total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 974 MB storm force low moved into the central Gulf of Alaska on the afternoon of Dec 13 with lots of warm moist air moving over the arctic front which was located near Berners Bay. By the afternoon of the 14th a secondary low moved into the eastern Gulf making landfall near Cape Fairweather then redeveloped over the Yukon overnight. The flow regime became southwest on the 15th with some cooler air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skagway Customs observed a storm total of 7 inches of new snow. Winds were as high as 56 mph on the 14th and 54 mph on the 15th for near blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low with a central pressure of 936 mb developed off Japan and moved to the western Aleutian Islands. As this low approached winds in the western Aleutians reached high wind levels even though the system was slowly weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shemya (PASY) reported winds over 70 mph from around 11 PM AKST January 15 through 9 AM AKST January 16th. The peak of the event was 74 mph. No reported damage or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed at Bishop Rock on the Yukon River, downstream of Galena on the 26th and caused water to back up. flooding began during mid-morning of the 27th and flood waters did not recede until the 31st. All structures were impacted by the high water except those significantly elevated or protected by the dike which surrounds the airport. Almost all roads were washed out...with boats being used for emergency transportation. Power was out to most of the city. Many residents were evacuated to nearby villages or to Fairbanks. Ice chunks knocked some houses off their foundation. Other buildings had up to 8 feet of water. At 1830AKST on the 29th water started slowly falling as the ice jam eased a bit. It was not until the morning of the 31st when water levels fell below flood stage. Damage amounts are estimated and include 14 homes destroyed, 90 homes with major damage, and 55 homes with minor damage, and nearly 6 million dollars for emergency response and public infrastructure.

CZ_NAME: NE. SLOPES OF THE ERN AK RNG
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska and an associated warm front produced copious amounts of moisture for the Alaska Range from May 17-18th. Heavy snow developed|from the Richardson Highway westward on the afternoon of the 17th and spread east during that night.  Just over one foot of snow was reported at the Black Rapids Lodge on the Richardson Highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over one foot of snow was reported at the Black Rapids lodge by friday evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed downstream of Eagle on the 17th causing water levels to rise rapidly. Flooding was the second worst on record...not approaching the levels of 2009. Several structures along Front Street sustained major damage, as well as some homes several miles upstream of Eagle. The road to Eagle Village was covered with large ice pans and ice chunks for a considerable distance. The old Eagle Village site was covered with both ice and water, though residents had moved away from that site following the 2009 flood. Damage amounts estimated major damage to homes as well as Emergency Response costs and road repair work.

Flooding occurred at Tok on the evening of the 1st through the morning of the 3rd due to excessive snowmelt feeding the Tok River. The Alaska Stoves Campground was inundated, along with some local streets and several buildings on the airport. Several parking lots were covered with water. The Alaska Highway however, was not affected. Two homes were reported with some water in them; damage amount is an estimate based on a minor amount of damage from the flood water.

An ice jam formed downstream of Alakanuk causing water to rise in Alakanuk and Emmonak on the 4th into the morning of the 6th. Water flowed over the road to the airport in each village....it was 2-3 feet deep in spots at Alakanuk. The taxi way at the Emmonak airport was washed out. At Alakanuk, 3 to 4 homes had water in them and the residents of these homes were evacuated. The sewage lagoon was inundated and it was noted that some propane tanks to home heating were floating in town. By mid-morning on the 6th, the ice jam had moved out and river levels had fallen in both communities.

Ice jams combined with warm temperatures accelerated snow melt in the Copper River basin in late May. This caused the Gulkana river to reach moderate flood stage at a number of locations along the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The GRSA2 river Gauge reported flood conditions beginning the afternoon of May 24 and persisting to the end of the month. Flood stage is 12 ft and the crest was on May 30th at 14.64 ft. This level ties for the second highest crest on record for this gauge. The BLM campground at Sourdough Creek received some damage from both water and large chunks of ice. Pictures show some damage but BLM was unable to give a damage estimate when asked. Made gross estimate based on damage from pictures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice jams combined with warm temperatures accelerated snow melt in the Copper River basin in late May. This caused the Gulkana river to reach moderate flood stage at a number of locations along the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gulkana River at the Richardson Highway pushed up past flood levels the morning of May 25th and reached a crest on May 30th around midnight of 12.13 ft. Moderate flood level for this gauge is 10.5 ft. The river observer at Gulkana reported on May 25th: Ice run last night flooded parking area/boat launch area at bridge. Stranded some ice over bank. The river remained above flood stage into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam released upstream of Circle City and sent a surge of water and ice through the city during the 19th, causing major flooding by inundating the city with 5 to 8 feet of water. Most structures were flooded except for the school which was slightly more elevated. All residents were evacuated to higher ground. The area upstream from the boat launch on the slough was most affected as the water levels brought a considerable amount of ice right into the homes in that area. Elsewhere, the main effects were water damage. One vehicle carrying village elders tried to drive through the flooded street to safety and went off of the road into the ditch. They were rescued in the bucket of a front loader and taken to safety.  Water levels according to a long-time resident were the 2nd highest in over 50 years, with only the floods in 1989 more severe. Water levels began falling in the late afternoon and receded below flood level by mid-evening on the 19th. Damage amounts are estimated, and include repairs to 8 homes with major damage and 7 homes with minor damage, along with nearly 1 million dollars expended in emergency response and road repair.

Rapid snowmelt at higher elevations of the Salcha River basin caused levels to rise rapidly producing minor flooding along the river. The event likely began on the upper stretch of the river during the morning of the 25th, and concluded at tle lower end of the river entering the Tanana River around noon on the 26th. No structures were reported as impacted.

An ice jam formed on the Koyukuk River at Hughes during the early morning of the 27th and then broke apart on the morning of the 29th. River water rose significantly on the 27th, flooding the old/lower town site and cutting it off from the upper town site. Water also surrounded the airport by early on the 28th though the airport was still usable. Damage amounts are estimated and includes 2 homes with major damage and 7 homes with minor damage, along with $115,000 to repair public infrastructure.

A cold front brought in unseasonable cold air to south central Alaska on May 17 and 18th. In addition there was a weak low in the Gulf of Alaska that moved northward into Prince William Sound as the cold front approached. The result was the front becoming stationary over the Anchorage area with over running moisture from the low that began as rain on Friday May 17th, but turned to snow through the course of the afternoon and persisted into Saturday morning.|This was an unusual event in the lateness of the storm in the season as it was the second latest date for accumulating snowfall in Anchorage on record. This storm also made the 2012-2013 snow season the longest duration one on record as there were 232 days (inclusive) between the first and last snowfalls of the season.|Although the official measurement of the Anchorage WFO was only .4 inches for the event, it was the anomaly. Snow reports around the rest of the Anchorage bowl ranged from 1.9 to 5.0 inches at lower elevations with numerous reports of 6 inches or more at elevations over 1000 ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured snowfall 3.5 inches in Southeast Anchorage at 700 ft elevation.|Measured snowfall 6.0 inches near Upper DeArmoun Rd at 1300 ft elevation. |Measured snowfall 6.3 inches mile 6.4 of Eagle River Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought in unseasonable cold air to south central Alaska on May 17 and 18th. In addition there was a weak low in the Gulf of Alaska that moved northward into Prince William Sound as the cold front approached. The result was the front becoming stationary over the Anchorage area with over running moisture from the low that began as rain on Friday May 17th, but turned to snow through the course of the afternoon and persisted into Saturday morning.|This was an unusual event in the lateness of the storm in the season as it was the second latest date for accumulating snowfall in Anchorage on record. This storm also made the 2012-2013 snow season the longest duration one on record as there were 232 days (inclusive) between the first and last snowfalls of the season.|Although the official measurement of the Anchorage WFO was only .4 inches for the event, it was the anomaly. Snow reports around the rest of the Anchorage bowl ranged from 1.9 to 5.0 inches at lower elevations with numerous reports of 6 inches or more at elevations over 1000 ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured snowfall 4.4 inches near Eagle River.|Measured snowfall 5.0 inches in Chugiak.|Measured snowfall 9.1 inches on Upper Hiland Rd elevation 2165 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 20th a surge of water and ice from the upstream river ice breakup moved to near Fort Yukon causing flooding of low-lying areas. A large sheet of ice became stuck 12 miles upstream of Fort Yukon causing widespread flooding of low-lying areas to persist. This affected the ball field, the old tank farm, the area surrounding the tribal hall, several homes, and one of several access roads to the airport. Around midnight on the 21st, the ice jam partially released though river levels did not rise significantly in town as a result. Later on the 22nd river levels began to slowly fall and by the morning of the 23rd levels were below flood stage. Damage amounts are estimated, and include repairs to 4 homes with major damage and 1 home with minor damage, along with affected roads, buildings and emergency response needs.

Rapid snowmelt at higher elevations of the Salcha River basin caused levels to rise rapidly producing minor flooding along the river. The event likely began on the upper stretch of the river during the morning of the 25th, and concluded at tle lower end of the river entering the Tanana River around noon on the 26th. No structures were reported as impacted.

The event likely began on the upper stretch of the river during the morning of the 25th, and concluded at tle lower end of the river entering the Tanana River around noon on the 26th. An ice jam formed 1/2 mile downstream from Buckland on the Buckland River during the afternoon of the 26th causing flooding in the village of Buckland beginning the morning of the 27th. Water levels dropped on the morning of the 29th below flood levels. During the event the lower part of the village was flooded and several homes were surrounded by water. Damage amounts are estimated.
Clear skies and calm winds caused the first freeze of the season at Gustavus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gustavus AWOS observed 32F for a number of hours from midnight to 0600 AKDT on the morning of 9/25. The observed minimum temperature was 30F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a record breaking cold and late spring in South Central Alaska, a warm upper level ridge built over the region in early June. This caused rapid snow melt which produced floods in the Copper River basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gulkana River gauge at Sourdough Creek (GRSA2) entered June in flood stage and did not recede under action stage until the morning of June 12th. This continued to impact the Sourdough Creek BLM campground from the ice jam flood documented in May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a record breaking cold and late spring in South Central Alaska, a warm upper level ridge built over the region in early June. This caused rapid snow melt which produced floods in the Copper River basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gulkana River gauge at the Paxon Lake outlet (GPOA2) was in flood stage when June began due to snow melt. It gradually receded to below flood level around the 21st of June. The flood on this section of the river also damaged buildings and a bridge at the Gulkana Fish Hatchery on June 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant amount of snowmelt runoff combined with ice jams caused the Colville River to go overbank on the morning of the 5th at Colville Village, where an NWS Cooperative Weather Observer resides. This event created the highest water levels there since the spring of 2000, and the second highest since official observations from a staff gauge began in 1986. The airstrip was underwater and several inches of water was present in all the lower elevation structures. The main lodge which was a little bit elevated was surrounded by water.

A 993 MB gale force triple point low moved into the central Gulf of Alaska on the morning of the 19th forcing warm moist air over cold air at the surface in the Panhandle. This system brought heavy snow to much of SE Alaska including the northern Panhandle, Yakutat, and Hyder.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gustavus has two observations of heavy snow: 11 inches storm total and 7.7 inches storm total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center of 976 mb near the Pribilofs on the afternoon of the 12th moved  north to the Bering Strait 0900AKST on the 14th weakening to 986 mb, then continued north to the central Chukchi sea through the morning of the 15th, weakening to 1002 mb.  A series of weather fronts associated with the low moved northeast over northern Alaska on the 13th through 15th, at first pushing warmer air north then followed by cooling on the 15th.||Zone 201: Blizzard conditions occurred at Wainwright, Point Lay, and Atqasuk. At Atqasuk, the wind gusted as high as 56 kt (64 mph) around noon on the 13th.||Zone 202: Blizzard conditions occurred at Barrow From about noon on the 13th through mid-morning on the 14th, where the visibility was 1/4 mile or less in snow and blowing snow. The wind gusted as high as 51 kt (59 mph) in the late morning of the 13th.||Zone 203: Blizzard conditions occurred at Deadhorse, Nuiqsut, Alpine, and Kuparuk. The wind gusted as high as 51 kt (59 mph) at Deadhorse on the morning of the 13th. ||Zone 206; Blizzard conditions likely occurred over the far northern portion of the zone throughout the 13th, as inferred from conditions reported by observing platforms in the adjacent zone 203.||Zone 209: Blizzard conditions occurred at Selawik, where the visibility was around 1/4 mile in blowing snow during the evening of the 12th.  Near-blizzard conditions occurred at Kotzebue during this time.| |Zone 213: Blizzard conditions occurred at Gambell during the evening of the 12th, as indicated by the AWOS. Blowing snow reduced visibility to 1/4 mile or less. ||Zone 215: Rain was reported at Russian Mission. During the afternoon of the 12th 0.21 inches fell.||Zone 217: Near blizzard conditions were likely at Shungnak where the AWSS reported visibilities as low as 1/4 mile, but only for about an hour. Additionally, the wind gusted as high as 52 knots (60 mph) at Shungnak.  ||Zone 219: Heavy snow was reported at Bettles, where a storm total of 14 inches was reported by the FAA contract weather observer.||Zone 221: Rain fell on the 14th over the western portions of the zone, with Nenana ASOS receiving 0.16 inches and Clear Sky Lodge Co-operative Observer reporting 0.29 inches.||Zone 222: Freezing Rain fell in the Fairbanks area. The Airport received approximately 0.15 inches on the 14th. The amount of ice that developed on the roads prompted school officials to close school on the 15th.||Zone 224: Northway ASOS reported 0.13 inch of rain on the morning of the 14th.

A 989 mb low south of Attu the afternoon of February 28 moved eastward and rapidly deepened 25 mb overnight to 964 mb the morning of March 1. It then began to slow its forward movement and slowly weaken as it cut a path just south of the Aleutian Islands through the evening of March 2nd. This storm brought Blizzard conditions to parts of the Aleutians and northward to the Pribilof Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the main low crossed just south of the Aleutian Islands the occluded front moved just south of the Pribilof Islands. This created a strong pressure gradient and area of strong lift over the Pribilof Islands. St Paul Island entered Blizzard conditions a little before midnight and peaked around 3 AM AKST when visibility continued to be 1/4 mile in blowing snow driven by Easterly winds gusting to 45 mph. St George Island was near blizzard conditions that night with nearly five hours of 1/2 mile visibility reports to go along with winds gusting to 50 mph. There were no reports of damage or injuries from either Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A North Pacific low rapidly deepened from 981 MB at 00Z on February 5th to 950 MB by 18Z on February 5th. This storm then maintained strength in the lower to mid 950 mb range through 00Z on February 7th. The occluded front associated with this low first brought blizzard conditions to the Western Aleutians and then to the Pribilof Islands the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the strong low approached the Western Aleutian Islands Shemya (PASY) reported blizzard conditions for four hours during the afternoon of February 5th. The peak of this event was at 4:56 PM AKST when the visibility was 1/8 mile in heavy snow and blowing snow that was produced by Northeast winds that were 59 MPH gusting to 72 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A North Pacific low rapidly deepened from 981 MB at 00Z on February 5th to 950 MB by 18Z on February 5th. This storm then maintained strength in the lower to mid 950 mb range through 00Z on February 7th. The occluded front associated with this low first brought blizzard conditions to the Western Aleutians and then to the Pribilof Islands the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the occluded front approached the Pribilof Islands, East to Northeast winds increased and caused blizzard conditions at both St Paul and St George Islands. The peak of the event on St Paul Island was at 12:53 PM AKST Feb 6th with East winds 40 gusting to 49 MPH. Visibility was 1/4 mile at this time. On St George Island the peak was at 2:42 PM AKST with East-Northeast winds 32 gusting 42 MPH producing visibility of 1/4 mile in snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front pushed northward along the Eastern Kenai Peninsula and through Prince William Sound on February 7th. This brought Blizzard Conditions to the Portage area during the day on February 7th. By late in the afternoon the front had passed through the region and winds quickly diminished.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blizzard began around 9:48 AM AKST February 7th in Portage (PATO) and ended by 5:29 PM that afternoon. The peak of the event was at 2:53 PM with East winds at 29 gusting to 51 MPH. The visibility was 1/4 mile in Snow and Blowing Snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low analyzed at 962 mb and over 500 miles south of the Alaska Peninsula the afternoon of February 8th moved northward and over the Alaska Peninsula at 969 MB by the afternoon on February 9th. It then elongated over southwest Alaska and developed a secondary low near Norton Sound the morning of February 10th. In the Gulf of Alaska the front associated with this low was occluding as it moved northward. As this occluded front moved on shore along the North Gulf Coast it brought strong winds through Turnagain Arm and to higher elevations in the Chugach and Kenai Mountains. Transportable snow in Thompson Pass allowed blizzard conditions to develop in the strong winds. As this system moved through the area it caused Winter Storm conditions to develop in multiple zones from the Pribilof Islands to Thompson Pass near Valdez. That is a distance of nearly 900 miles and warnings were in effect at one time or another for almost this entire distance as the storm progressed. The southwest Alaska coast blizzard warnings did not verify due to the short duration of the blowing snow. However there were numerous locations that did receive a short period of white out conditions in the Kuskokwim Delta region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the low moved into southwest mainland Alaska the pressure gradient tightened over the Pribilof Islands as snow and more cold air was entrained into the area. This resulted in around 12 hours of continuous blizzard conditions in St Paul and 6 hours in St George. The peak of the event was on February 9th at 11:53 AM AKST when St Paul reported 1/16 mile visibility in snow and blowing snow with North winds 40 gusting 46 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low analyzed at 962 mb and over 500 miles south of the Alaska Peninsula the afternoon of February 8th moved northward and over the Alaska Peninsula at 969 MB by the afternoon on February 9th. It then elongated over southwest Alaska and developed a secondary low near Norton Sound the morning of February 10th. In the Gulf of Alaska the front associated with this low was occluding as it moved northward. As this occluded front moved on shore along the North Gulf Coast it brought strong winds through Turnagain Arm and to higher elevations in the Chugach and Kenai Mountains. Transportable snow in Thompson Pass allowed blizzard conditions to develop in the strong winds. As this system moved through the area it caused Winter Storm conditions to develop in multiple zones from the Pribilof Islands to Thompson Pass near Valdez. That is a distance of nearly 900 miles and warnings were in effect at one time or another for almost this entire distance as the storm progressed. The southwest Alaska coast blizzard warnings did not verify due to the short duration of the blowing snow. However there were numerous locations that did receive a short period of white out conditions in the Kuskokwim Delta region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RWIS wind sensor at McHugh Creek (SMCA2) reported winds over 70 mph from 10:50 AM February 9th through 12:20 PM that day. The highest wind gust was measured at 76 MPH at 11:20 AM. No reports of damage or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 989 mb low south of Attu the afternoon of February 28 moved eastward and rapidly deepened 25 mb overnight to 964 mb the morning of March 1. It then began to slow its forward movement and slowly weaken as it cut a path just south of the Aleutian Islands through the evening of March 2nd. This storm brought Blizzard conditions to parts of the Aleutians and northward to the Pribilof Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Adak AWOS (PADK) and the NWS surface analysis was used for this event verification. The event took place in the eastern part of zone 191 and PADK is the closest station that was in a similar section of the storm as analysed by NCEP/OPC on their surface analysis. Therefore the 8 hours of Blizzard conditions reported on Adak are likely similar to what was occurring in the eastern part of zone 191. The timing was also estimated from the PADK observations and the surface analysis. PADK reported 1/4 mile visibility for around 8 hours with Northwest winds gusting up to 77 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving low crossed the Eastern Bering Sea and passed just north of the Kuskokwim Delta on March 9th. The strong south to southwest gradient over the region in conjunction with the lifting from the low produced blizzard conditions in a number of communities in Southwest Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nunivak Island village of Mekoryuk (PAMY) dropped down to blizzard conditions from just after noon on March 9th until a little after 4 PM. Visibility was consistently at 1/4 mile with winds gusting from the south-southwest at 34 to 39 mph. There were no reports of injuries or damages in this event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alaska DOT reported blizzard conditions from 7 AM Saturday, February 9th through 6 AM Sunday, February 10th in Thompson Pass. The peak of this event was around 10 AM Saturday when DOT reported white out conditions in Thompson Pass with <1/4 mile visibility in heavy snow. At that time winds were southeast at 25 gusting 40 mph. No reports of damage or injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: False Pass (PAKF) began to experience blizzard conditions around 10 AM on February 9th when the visibility was 1/4 mile in heavy snow and southwest winds were gusting to 51 mph. These blizzard conditions were last observed at 1:35 PM that afternoon. Winds in this event were particularly strong with gusts over 60 mph on three separate observations. The strongest wind was a 72 mph gust at 1:35 PM. There were no reports of damage or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather pattern set up over southern Alaska February 19th through 23rd where a series of triple point lows developed along the jet stream south of the Alaska Peninsula. These lows each deepened rapidly and tracked into the Northern Gulf of Alaska. These lows were generally in the 970 to 990 mb range as they impacted southern Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the 976 mb low passed south of Bristol Bay during the day on February 19th it put the zone in cold air advection which enhanced the already strong pressure gradient and dynamic lifting. This resulted in at least six location reporting heavy and/or blowing snow of 1/4 mile visibility. King Salmon recorded its daily snowfall record with a value of 5.7 inches. King Salmon also reported Blizzard or near Blizzard conditions for three hours. Dillingham reported Blizzard conditions from around noon until almost 5 PM AKST that afternoon with the most severe condition at 12:46 PM with north winds 31 gusting 43 MPH producing 1/4 mile visibility in heavy snow and blowing snow. Manokotak (PAMB) also reported blizzard conditions from around noon until almost 6 PM with the most severe conditions being at 2:56 PM with Northeast winds 34 gusting 41 mph producing 1/4 mile visibility in snow and blowing snow. |There were no reports of damage or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong occluded front associated with a mid-970s mb low approached the Western Aleutian Islands from the south. This set up strong winds and snow across the Western Aleutian Islands which resulted in a blizzard warning for that area. This low pressure system is a different storm system than the one producing winter storms over other parts of southern Alaska this same day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Shemya (PASY) ASOS reported five hours of borderline blizzard conditions with the peak of the storm producing 1/2 mile visibility in moderate snow driven by Northeast winds sustained at 39 MPH and gusting to 49 MPH. Given the lack of terrain on Shemya, and the distance from the Bering Sea to the ASOS being less than 2 miles, it reasons that nearby Attu Island with its much larger mass and significant terrain saw conditions much lower than at Shemya. However, the lack of any observations from Attu prevents confirmation of this. There were no reports of damage or injuries from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A triple point low south of the Central Aleutian Islands the afternoon of Sunday, February 17 rapidly strengthened as it moved northward into the Bering Sea. In 24 hours it deepened 30 mb from 988 mb Sunday afternoon to 958 mb on Monday afternoon. The occluded front associated with this low approached the Pribilof Islands and produced blizzard conditions. Aside from the Pribilof Islands, the brunt of this strong storm remained over the Bering Sea and did not move over land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The occluded front that was moving slowly through the Eastern Bering Sea from the south produced blizzard conditions on both St Paul and St George Island. St George reported the more severe conditions with 1/4 mile visibility in East winds sustained at 56 mph and gusting to 72 mph in the peak of the event. St George also experienced blizzard conditions for 13 hours. St Paul reported blizzard conditions for 5 hours with the most severe conditions being 1/4 mile visibility and East winds sustained at 45 mph and gusting to 60 mph. While there were no reported injuries and no reported damage, school was canceled for Monday, February 18. Government offices and the AC store were closed until noon that day due to snow drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong 1045 mb high pressure center over the Arctic Ocean produced strong winds along the North Slope. Temperatures of 30 to 45 below zero combined with a northeast wind of around 15 mph to produce wind chills as low as 64 below at the Nuiqsut ASOS in zone 203. A wind chill of 72 below was recorded at the Anaktuvuk Pass AWOS in zone 206.

High pressure in the interior of the state and low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska produced strong winds through Thompson Pass near Valdez. Falling and recently fallen snow provided the source of the blowing snow that reached blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were expected in Thompson Pass from 1 AM Monday, February 18 through late that evening due to strong winds developing through the pass and recent snowfall providing transportable snow. At 3:40 AM the state DOT plowing the pass reported less than 1/4 mile visibility in moderate snow and northeast winds sustained at 33 mph and gusting between 40 and 55 mph. The report from the DOT camp at 6 AM had light snow with 5 plus miles visibility and Northeast winds 10 gusting 30 mph. Due to the sporadic observations and the fact that conditions can be much better at the DOT camp, the timing of this event is an estimate. Looking at surface maps from this time it appears reasonable that the blizzard peaked from around 1 AM to 5 AM on the 18th, but conditions may have remained much lower through the pass itself well into the day. Blizzard criteria is estimated to have been reached at 4 AM on February 18th. No reports of damage or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather pattern set up over southern Alaska February 19th through 23rd where a series of triple point lows developed along the jet stream south of the Alaska Peninsula. These lows each deepened rapidly and tracked into the Northern Gulf of Alaska. These lows were generally in the 970 to 990 mb range as they impacted southern Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a typical Blizzard situation in Portage Valley with strong east to southeast winds coming through Portage pass and heavy snowfall with the passing of a front from the south. This event began with light snow and strong winds. The wind came down as snowfall rate was increasing. While the winds dropped below blizzard criteria before the end of the event the heavy snowfall rate combined with the winds that were occurring likely kept white-out conditions in the area for the duration of the event. There were no reported injuries and no reported damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 961 mb low near the Western Aleutian Islands the morning of February 22 moved to just south of the Pribilof Islands that night and then slowly weakened as it continued to move eastward. The structure of the occluded front originating with this low was such that it kept a tight gradient over St Paul Island much longer than St George Island. This low is a separate low than the ones referenced in the episode Series of fast moving lows impacting Bristol Bay to Prince William Sound Feb 19-23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This long duration Blizzard on St Paul Island was produced by the occluded front remaining just south of the Islands on February 22 through the early morning hours of the 23rd. By late morning the low was moving east of the Island and while this kept the front from passing through St Paul it tightened the back side gradient around the western edge of the low that afternoon and brought conditions back to blizzard levels after a slight reprieve the morning of the 23rd. The most severe conditions for St Paul were at 10:53 PM February 22 when visibility was 1/4 mile in blowing snow driven by Northeast winds sustained at 39 and gusting to 47 MPH.|Neither the Village Public Safety Officer for St Paul or for St George reported any damage or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low passed south of the Alaska Peninsula on February 25th as it made its way to the Gulf of Alaska. This low was analyzed between 974 and 978 mb at this time and the Alaska Peninsula was in the cold sector of the storm. This allowed strong cold air advection and dynamic lifting to increase the snow and blowing snow to blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The visibility in Cold Bay (PACD) reached 1/4 mile for the first time at 1:53 AM AKST on the 25th and blizzard criteria was met at 4:53 AM. Blizzard conditions persisted until 4:53 PM that afternoon in Cold Bay. Cold Bay winds were from the North and Northwest and were very consistent for the entire event; gusting between 35 and 41 MPH the entire time. |King Cove (PAVC) developed blizzard conditions at 3:56 AM and blizzard criteria was reached at 6:56 AM. These conditions are estimated to have ended around 7:41 PM that evening. This is an estimate based on the ASOS sensor at PAVC missing the visibility for the afternoon and evening portion of this event. However, judging by wind speeds, precipitation type, and clouds ceilings, in addition to the nearby Cold Bay observation this looks to be a reasonable estimate. Winds in King Cove were quite strong with peak gusts over 75 MPH from 6 AM through the end of the event. The strong peak wind gust was at 11:56 AM when it reached 90 MPH. |The Cold Bay WSO did not report any damage or injuries in either location for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong 1045 mb high pressure center over the Arctic Ocean produced moderate winds along the North Slope. Temperatures around 25 below zero combined with a northeast wind of around 25 mph to produce wind chills as low as 64 below at the Barter Island AWSS on the afternoon of the 22nd through the morning of the 23rd.

Strong high pressure over the Arctic Ocean and low pressure in the Bering Strait produced blizzard conditions at a few locations along the Arctic Coast during the 23rd. At Wainwright in zone 201, the visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less. There was a peak wind gust of 36 kt (41 mph) at the Wainwright ASOS. At Deadhorse in zone 203, the visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile in blowing snow, with winds gusting around 35 mph at times.

Strong high pressure over Russia and low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska produced blizzard conditions on Saint Lawrence Island and the Bering Strait from 1456AKST on the 4th through 1850AKST on the 5th. The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less at Gambell. There was a peak wind gust of 41 kt (47 mph) at the Gambell AWOS. Blizzard conditions were also observed at Wales on the afternoon of the 5th. The visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less. There was a peak wind gust of 39kt (45 mph) at the Wales AWOS.

Strong high pressure over Russia and low pressure over the Seward Peninsula produced blizzard conditions along the Bering Strait during the evening hours of the 10th|through the early morning hours of the 11th. The visibility was frequently reduced to one quarter mile or less. There was a peak wind gust of 55 kt (63 mph) at the Teller AWOS. Blizzard conditions were also observed at Wales. The visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less. There was a peak wind gust of 43kt (50 mph) at the Wales AWOS.

A 984 mb low moved north over the eastern Bering Sea on the 11th and gradually weakened by early on the 12th north of the Bering Strait. This system brought blizzard conditions to Wales, and near-blizzard conditions to Gambell, Savoonga, and Teller. The Wales AWOS reported a peak gust to 45 kts (52 mph) during the event.

A strong 972 mb low pressure system entered the Southern Bering Strait early in the morning on January 19th. The strong pressure gradient and snowfall produced blizzard conditions at Gambell on Saint Lawrence Island in zone 213, and at Point Hope on the Chukchi Sea coast, the wind gusted as high as 58 kt (67 mph).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions reported at Gambell AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from a low pressure system in the Gulf of Alaska produced heavy snow for eastern and southern interior from the evening of the 23rd through the 25th. Over one foot of snow fell along the Richardson Highway from the 24th through the 25th. In Salcha up to 8 inches of snow was reported by a storm spotter. At Eagle in zone 224, near the Canada border, the Cooperative Observer reported 10 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to Two feet of snow reported by Alaska DOT. Highway reopened on Saturday the 26th.

This produced Blizzard conditions over both St Paul Island and St George Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions occurred from late afternoon/early evening of February 2nd on both Pribilof Islands and persisted into the middle of the afternoon the next day. The peak of the event on St George was at 5:03 PM February 2nd when they were 1/4 mile visibility in blowing snow and North winds 38 mph gusting to 51 mph. The peak on St Paul was 11:53 AM February 3rd with 1/4 mile visibility in blowing snow and North winds 39 MPH gusting to 45 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed along the west coast of Alaska between a 1045 high over the Arctic and 977 mb low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska. Local blizzard conditions with blowing snow and visibility one quarter mile or less at times were reported at Wainwright ASOS during the afternoon and evening hours of 5th.

A typical winter pattern set up with High pressure and cold air inland and a Low pressure center in the northern Gulf of Alaska. The pressure and thermal gradients were sufficient to accelerate winds to warning levels through Thompson pass. Winds were close to warning levels in the City of Valdez.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thompson Pass gusted over 70 mph for around three hours the morning of April 25. The peak gust was at 10:43 AM AKDT when a peak wind gust of 77 mph was recorded at the RHYA2 Road Weather Information System sensor run by the DOT. The Valdez airport and Valdez harbor both reported peak wind gusts of 67 mph that morning. There were no reports of damage or injuries.

A low pressure center developing over the Mackenzie Delta on the 10th likely created blizzard conditions at Barter Island and Kaktovik...where the AWOS indicated the visibility was one-quarter mile or less and the wind gusted as high as 53 knots (61 mph).

A high pressure center over the western Chukchi Sea and low pressure over the northwest interior of Alaska created an area of strong winds over the northern Chukchi Sea coast. Blizzard conditions were likely on the 9th through the early morning hours of the 10th at Kivalina where the ASOS indicated the visibility was one-quarter mile or less and the wind gusted between 40 and 50 mph.
For two cold, calm, and clear nights in a row, temperatures dropped below freezing to threaten Spring growth of sensitive plants.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Gustavus observed a minimum temperature or 27F on the morning of 5/20 around 0500 AKST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of Wednesday, March 6th, there was a 960 mb low with occluded front centered about 400 miles south of Dutch Harbor. This low continued to strengthen some as it progressed northward reaching a peak of 954 mb just south of the Alaska Peninsula on the morning of March 7th. This storm then started to weaken as it moved into Bristol Bay and then inland on March 8th. This storm brought high winds to the Anchorage area as well as blizzard conditions to parts of Southwest Alaska and the Alaska Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the low moved into Bristol Bay the pressure gradient increased over the Alaska Peninsula and also ushered in colder air to the area. Snow and blowing snow in Cold Bay (PACD) dropped the visibility to 1/4 mile by mid morning on March 8th and this persisted until mid afternoon. West winds during this period were gusting between 43 and 49 mph.|There were no reports of damage or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of Wednesday, March 6th, there was a 960 mb low with occluded front centered about 400 miles south of Dutch Harbor. This low continued to strengthen some as it progressed northward reaching a peak of 954 mb just south of the Alaska Peninsula on the morning of March 7th. This storm then started to weaken as it moved into Bristol Bay and then inland on March 8th. This storm brought high winds to the Anchorage area as well as blizzard conditions to parts of Southwest Alaska and the Alaska Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strong pressure gradient ahead of the approaching occluded front produced high winds in both Turnagain Arm and along the Anchorage Hillside from mid evening March 7th to around 6 AM AKST on March 8th. The peak wind in Turnagain Arm was at McHugh Creek (SMCA2) where the wind gust was measured at 82 mph at 8:30 PM AKST on March 7th. The Glenn Alps (GAHA2) peak wind was measured at 95 mph at 11 PM on the 7th. There were no reports of damage or injuries from this event.

This storm then started to weaken as it moved into Bristol Bay and then inland on March 8th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the low passed south of the Eastern Aleutian Islands the pressure gradient rapidly increased over the Unalaska area. In addition to the strong winds snowfall increased and blowing snow reduced the visibility to 1/4 mile for almost four hours in Dutch Harbor (PADU). The peak of this event was around 5 PM AKST on March 6th when visibility remained at 1/4 mile in winds gusting to 44 mph and a peak gust that past hour to 64 mph. There were no reports of damage or injuries.

This storm then started to weaken as it moved into Bristol Bay and then inland on March 8th. This storm brought high winds to the Anchorage area as well as blizzard conditions to parts of Southwest Alaska and the Alaska Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: When the low and associated occluded front moved into Bristol Bay on March 7th the North to Northwest winds increased in conjunction with heavy snowfall. Blizzard conditions began just after midnight on the 7th and persisted into the middle of the evening. The most severe conditions on St Paul Island (PASN) were reported around 11 AM AKST with 1/4 mile visibility in blowing snow and Northwest winds sustained at 49 mph and gusting to 63 mph.

Port Heiden (PAPH), about 175 miles to the Southeast of Cape Newenham, reported four hours of warning level winds with the highest gust measured at 83 MPH. There were no reported injuries or damages in this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system moved along the Aleutian Islands with an occluded front ahead of it. As this front moved across the eastern Bering Sea and the southwest Alaska coast it produced blizzard conditions across that coast as well as over the Pribilof Islands.

The peak on St George Islands was 1/4SM visibility with winds gusting to 41 MPH. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 957 mb low pressure center in the south central Bering Sea moved northward on the 7th of March. The associated weather front moved across the eastern Interior on the night of the 6th through the 7th, The resulting strong chinook flow over the Alaska Range produced high winds in and near the Alaska Range. ||Zone 225: There was a peak wind gust to 55 kt (63 mph) at Otto Lake at 1615AKST on the afternoon of the 7th. Based on the peak wind gust at Otto Lake, it is likely that the wind gusted in excess of 70 mph at the Antler Creek DOT site at MP 244 along the Parks Highway.  ||Zone 226: The wind at the Texas Condo US Army mesonet  site gusted to 73 kts (84 mph).

A large low pressure system moved along the Aleutian Islands with an occluded front ahead of it. As this front moved across the eastern Bering Sea and the southwest Alaska coast it produced blizzard conditions across that coast as well as over the Pribilof Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions began at Tooksok Bay (PAOO) late in the afternoon of March 27th with visibility at 1/4 mile in snow and blowing snow produced by winds gusting to 35 mph at that time. Blizzard conditions were observed at times through the night and ended early the next morning. Blizzard conditions were also briefly reported at Mekoryuk (PAMY) late that night as well. The event peak was March 27 at 9:39 PM AKST when PAOO reported 1/4 mile visibility in snow and blowing snow with a wind gust of 40 MPH. There were no reports of damage or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center moved into Prince William Sound on Sunday March 24th and then became nearly stationary through Monday March 25th. Cold air at the surface and aloft moved into south central Alaska at the same time producing a long duration over-running snow event from the Anchorage bowl northward into the Susitna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Paradise valley observer reported 12 inches of snowfall from between 5 AM and 3 PM Monday March 25. Total for the storm was 17.0 inches at that location.|The Upper De Armoun site reported 23.5 inches of snow for the event.|Glenn Alps received 28.0 inches of snow for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of March 25th a 994 mb low crossed Portage Pass and entered Prince William Sound. As the low passed over Whittier the pressure gradient rapidly increased bringing in strong Westerly winds in addition to the heavy snow that had been falling. The low remained at close to the same strength and was nearly stationary through the day. It then began to significantly weaken around midnight on the 26th filling to 1003 mb by 12Z on the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow with 1/4 mile visibility had been falling in Whittier since the evening of the 24th but there was very little wind until the low passed East of Whittier. When this happened winds rapidly increased around 3 AM on the 25th and were gusting to 44 mph by 3:30 AM with continued 1/4 mile visibility in snow and blowing snow. The strongest peak wind gust was 53 MPH recorded around 4:30 AM. Peak wind gusts remained mostly in the 35 to 50 mph range for the next day but blizzard conditions abated around 3 AM on the 26th as snowfall diminished and the visibility improved. There were no reports of damage or injuries.

CZ_NAME: NE. SLOPES OF THE ERN AK RNG
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 957 mb low pressure center in the south central Bering Sea moved northward on the 7th of March. The associated weather front moved across the eastern Interior on the night of the 6th through the 7th, The resulting strong chinook flow over the Alaska Range produced high winds in and near the Alaska Range. ||Zone 225: There was a peak wind gust to 55 kt (63 mph) at Otto Lake at 1615AKST on the afternoon of the 7th. Based on the peak wind gust at Otto Lake, it is likely that the wind gusted in excess of 70 mph at the Antler Creek DOT site at MP 244 along the Parks Highway.  ||Zone 226: The wind at the Texas Condo US Army mesonet  site gusted to 73 kts (84 mph).

A strong pressure gradient between high pressure over the interior of the state and low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska resulted in strong winds through the channeled terrain of Thompson Pass. Recent snowfall in that area created a large amount of transportable snow in the pass which created blizzard conditions on April 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed early in the morning on Friday, April 12th and by 4 AM AKDT the DOT reported blizzard conditions. Visibility reported by DOT was less than 100 feet from mile 23 to mile 27 of the Richardson Highway. Blizzard conditions persisted through around 5 PM AKDT and then rapidly improved by 6 PM AKDT. The event peak was at 4 AM to 6 AM when there was 100 mile visibility in blowing snow driven by north winds gusting to 60 MPH. There were no reports of damage or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 989 mb low south of Attu the afternoon of February 28 moved eastward and rapidly deepened 25 mb overnight to 964 mb the morning of March 1. It then began to slow its forward movement and slowly weaken as it cut a path just south of the Aleutian Islands through the evening of March 2nd. This storm brought Blizzard conditions to parts of the Aleutians and northward to the Pribilof Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Adak (PADK) AWOS reported 1/4 mile visibility with extremely strong Northwest winds with this system. During the event the winds gusted between 58 and 77 MPH the entire time. The peak of the storm was around 5:30 AM on March 2 with 1/4 mile visibility in Northwest wind gusting to 77 MPH. No reports of damage or injuries from the City of Adak Facebook page.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving low crossed the Eastern Bering Sea and passed just north of the Kuskokwim Delta on March 9th. The strong south to southwest gradient over the region in conjunction with the lifting from the low produced blizzard conditions in a number of communities in Southwest Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bethel (PABE) reported southerly winds that were gusting between 38 and 43 mph. From around 6 PM until midnight AKST, these winds combined with snow and blowing snow reduced the visibility to 1/4 mile. There were no reported injuries or damages from this event in Bethel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure centered near the Arctic Coast combined with a low in the Gulf of Alaska produced a strong pressure gradient over Southern Alaska on March 12. This strong pressure gradient produced warning level winds in the Matanuska Valley and in various places along the north coast of the Gulf of Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds coming out of the Matanuska River valley reached the intersection of the Glenn and Parks highways near Palmer. The Glenn Highway milepost 35 weather information sensor reported peak wind gusts of 78 and 84 mph the afternoon of March 12. The wind blew down trees and knocked down a traffic sign 6 miles SW of Palmer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure centered near the Arctic Coast combined with a low in the Gulf of Alaska produced a strong pressure gradient over Southern Alaska on March 12. This strong pressure gradient produced warning level winds in the Matanuska Valley and in various places along the north coast of the Gulf of Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of high winds across the entire Valdez area in this event. The gust recorder at the Valdez airport recorded a gust of 94 mph at 10:22 PM AKDT on March 12. Wind gusts to 74 mph were reported in the city of Valdez with Thompson Pass reaching 71 mph. The Valdez WSO reported no injuries or damages in the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure centered near the Arctic Coast combined with a low in the Gulf of Alaska produced a strong pressure gradient over Southern Alaska on March 12. This strong pressure gradient produced warning level winds in the Matanuska Valley and in various places along the north coast of the Gulf of Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The very tight pressure gradient along the North Gulf Coast produced high winds through the Copper River Delta. There are no observing stations in the Delta and both the State Troopers and DOT did not go into the warning area but did report lots of blowing dust. Reasonable estimates of the timing and extent of the winds can be ascertained from the observations from Valdez where winds gusted to 74 mph in town and 94 mph at the airport. Therefore an estimate of 85 mph was used for this warning, and three hours were added to the end time from the ending in Valdez since the Copper River Delta winds last longer than in Valdez in this pattern.

Under a strong pressure gradient over northwest Alaska, blizzard conditions likely occurred at Kivalina during the morning and early afternoon of the 6th, as indicated by the AWOS where the visibility was one-quarter mile or less and winds gusted to 45 mph during this time.

A 998 mb low near Banks Island by 1000AKST on the 19th coupled with a strong 1038 mb high pressure center over the Chukchi Sea created a strong pressure gradient, providing strong winds and blizzard conditions along the eastern north slope during the afternoon of the 19th. ||Zone 203: Blizzard conditions were observed at Deadhorse from approximately 1200AKST through 1600AKST on the 19th. The visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow. There was a peak wind gust of 31 kt (36 mph) at the Deadhorse ASOS.||Zone 204: Blizzard conditions were observed at Barter Island from approximately 1200AKST through 1600AKST on the 19th. The visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow. There was a peak wind gust of 52 kt (60 mph) at the Barter Island AWOS.

A deep low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska and stationary high pressure over the North Slope produced windy conditions over the Fairbanks area on the evening of the 10th into the morning of the 13th. Although wind gusts did not reach advisory level at the official locations of Fairbanks International Airport, Fort Wainwright or Eielson Air Force Base, wind gusted as high as 32 kt (37 mph) at the Angel Creek RAWS.  The wind blew trees into power lines already coated with ice from previous storms since November, causing 4,500 homes to be without power on the night of the 11th and into the 12th. Damage amounts are unknown however some property damage from frozen pipes may have occurred.

A 984 mb low pressure center moved north from Bristol Bay to the Yukon Delta while weakening to 992 mb on the 20th. The associated weather front moved north over Norton Sound on the afternoon and evening producing snow at Nome totaling 7 inches before ending during the early morning of the 21st.

A 988 mb low pressure center moved into the western Bering Sea on the 5th of December. An associated warm front and warm air moved across the Bering strait on the 5th and over the west coast of Alaska on the 6th...then continued north over interior Alaska on the 6th and 7th before weakening.  This warm front spread rain or freezing rain to the west coast and many locations across the northern interior. ||Zone 207: Rain fell at Kivalina (0.12 inches accumulation) on the 6th of December. Though mostly rain, ground surfaces remained frozen, so the effect was the same as Freezing rain on ground surfaces. ||Zone 209: Rain fell at Kotzebue (0.34 inches accumulation) and at Norvik (.24 inches accumulation) on the 6th of December. Though mostly rain, ground surfaces remained frozen, so the effect was the same as Freezing rain on ground surfaces. ||Zone 210: Rain likely fell at several locations such as the Haycok RAWS (0.21 inch), Quartz Creek RAWS (0.24 inch), Serpentine RAWS (0.11 inch) and the Hoodoo RAWS (0.48 inch). Though mostly rain, ground surfaces remained frozen, so the effect was the same as Freezing rain on ground surfaces.||Zone 211: Nome received 0.27 inch of rain. White Mountain likely received 0.13 inch of rain; at both locations precipitation likely froze to the ground.||Zone 217: Rain likely fell at Ambler (0.43 inches accumulation) and at Shungnak (0.15 inches).  Though mostly rain, ground surfaces remained frozen, so the effect was the same as Freezing rain on ground surfaces.||Zone 218: An estimated 0.30 inch of freezing rain fell at Wiseman, based on the total liquid amount reported by the Co-operative Observer. ||Zone 219: The weather observer at Bettles reported 0.27 inch of freezing fain.||Zone 220: An estimated 0.20 inch of rain and freezing rain was likely received by some locations across this zone, based on reports from locations in nearby zones (Bettles, Wiseman, and Eagle). At Circle City, an unknown amount of rain was reported, though it was likely several tenths of an inch based on pictures sent.||Zone 224: Eagle received about 0.20 of an inch of freezing rain, based on the ASOS and Co-operative Observer reports.

A weather system moving across the area provided 2.5 to 5 inches of snow, with highest amounts northeast of Fairbanks, where the additional snow loaded power lines already covered with ice caused power outages to about 6000 electric utility members on the 9th into the 10th.

A strong 1045 mb high pressure centered 460 miles northeast of Wrangell Island coupled with a low pressure trough over the north slope of Alaska and the western Bering Sea, created snow, strong winds and blizzard conditions for Barrow during the morning hours of the 9th.||Zone 202: Blizzard conditions were observed at Barrow from approximately 0400AKST through 1300AKST on the 9th. The visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less in snow and blowing snow. There was a peak wind gust of 40 kt (46 mph) at the Barrow ASOS.

A 1044 mb high pressure over western Canada coupled with a low pressure center of 984 mb in the northern Gulf of Alaska produced a strong wind belt along the Tanana River from Dot Lake to west of Delta Junction. The strongest winds were measured near the Delta Junction during the late evening of the 21st and early morning of the 22nd where the highest gust was 64 kt (74 mph).

A series of weak low pressure systems persisted just off the coast in the Eastern Gulf while the wind aloft became south over the northern part of Lynn Canal. Cold air had settled into this area and the south flow aloft had plenty of moisture for a snow event just in time to impede traffic on the holiday weekend. Higher elevations had more snow than near sea level. ||Snow amounts: |Haines Customs = 5.5 inches	|White Pass = 8 inches (road obs)	|Skagway Customs = 2 inches	|Haines Junction got 7 cm	|Fraser got 8 cm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Customs measured 5.5 inches of new snow overnight which impeded the hloiday traffic over the Haines Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low in Bristol Bay on October 24th moved into northwest Alaska as the next low in a series of lows approached Bristol Bay. This set the pattern of lows in the eastern Bering Sea that kept South Central Alaska in a frontal zone for multiple days. This weather front had a strong pressure gradient along it and a long fetch of warm air with a subtropical connection. The storm peaked on October 27th and early on the 28th before finally pushing northward and out of the area the afternoon of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The airport at Seward measured 4.47 inches of rainfall between October 27-28, 2013. This caused the Lowell Creek diversion tunnel outlet stream to become filled with gravel from the creek that washed down from the mountainside in the heavy rainfall. The result was damage to the bridge over the road as flood waters topped the bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low in the Eastern Bering Sea produced a strong weather front that moved through South central Alaska from south to north October 12 through 13th. As the front approached the Anchorage area it produced high winds along Turnagain Arm and at higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The McHugh Creek weather station along Turnagain Arm reported several hours of high wind level winds with a peak gust of 83 mph. The Glenn Alps mesonet site recorded a peak wind gust of 84 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow hit the Klondike Highway on the night of 11/17 and the early morning of 11/28 just in time to impede holiday traffic. White Pass measured 8 inches of new snowfall (road obs), but Skagway Customs (Elev 962 FT) got 2 inches at a much lower elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air had persisted over SE Alaska for nearly a week when the flow aloft changed to SW and warm moist air began to lift over the Panhandle on 11/21. The arctic front was pushed slowly northward on 11/21 through 11/22 which became the focus of heavy snow. As the snow changed to rain, precipitation became freezing rain in a few locations. Strong winds began on the 11/22 though 11/23 with a switch over to rain. Temperatures warmed rapidly causing the snow pack to become very difficult to manage. This storm was notable in that the snowfall, freezing rain, and wind problems were widespread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow dump for Gustavus and Glacier Bay. Storm total 20 inches downtown Gustavus and 30 inches new snow for the Glacier Bay National Park area near Bartlett Cove.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds in the Anchorage area were strongest along the Hillside where the Glenn Alps mesonet site reported a gust of 105 mph, and a spotter in Bear Valley reported 106 mph.|Other reports included a measured 78 mph gust in Eagle river and an 81 mph gust near Potters Marsh. 400 - 500 persons lost electric power during this event. There were only a few reports of minor property damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 188 ft cell phone tower near Willow toppled over on October 25th due to a combination of factors, including weather. The tower was originally built in 1974 and according the the Mat-Su Borough: high winds combined with rain-soaked ground likely caused the collapse. The nearest observation locations that reported any winds only measured at maximum gust of 44 MPH at a Wasilla fire station (WSLA2). None of the observation stations near Willow showed any strong wind gusts at all. The channeled terrain near Willow where the tower was located may have received higher gusts than any of the observation locations, but anything over 60 mph appears unlikely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the first cold air outbreak with high outflow winds coming out of Taku Inlet and Lynn Canal. A moderate low pressure system was over the Southern Panhandle as a strong ridge of high pressure build over the Northern Yukon on 11/17. Peak wind for several locations in Downtown Juneau and southeastern Gastineau Channel ranged between 55 to 86 MPH into the early morning hours on 11/18. No damage was reported. Strong wind to 45 MPH persisted into 11/19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alaska Marine Lines observations (on the Marine Exchange) were gusting between 45 and 72 KT during the warning from around midnight on 11/17 to 0430 AKST on 11/18. Douglas Boat Harbor and the Marine Exchange Office observations were around 64 MPH peak wind during this time. Strong winds continued into 11/19.

A strengthening low crossed the Western Aleutian Islands into the Bering Sea early on September 24th. The tight pressure gradient around the western side of the low brought strong winds to the Central Aleutian Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Adak reported a wind gust to 63 KT, or 72 mph. Due to the location of Adak in regards to the storm and the extensive area without observations in the Central Aleutians it is reasonable to conclude that there were valid warning level winds in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, moist and slow moving weather front moved across the Southeast Alaska through the second week of December. The weather front had connection to tropical moisture and satellite imagery showed a well established atmospheric river pointed at the southern half of the Alaska panhandle. The front produced very heavy rainfall for multiple days to produce significant runoff into local hydroelectric dams which rose the water level above the emergency spillway for a few dam in the Ketchikan area. The uncontrollable flow from the dams and heavy precipitation rates produced very high water level in streams and river which produced some minor to moderate flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 5 days of heavy rain with very high rainfall totals from December 10-14. The Ketchikan airport received thirteen point forty six inches for a five day total with a daily record of five point zero four inches on the 11. It was the wettest five day total in December for the period of record going back to 1902. Other notable five day rainfall total from around the area was ten point twenty three inches at Ketchikan Powerhouse, twenty three point twenty three inches at Sivlis powerhouse. The excessive rainfall event produces enough runoff that area power generation reservoirs rose significantly. There was a fourteen foot rise at Upper Silvis lake and over five feet at Swan Lake. Ketchikan Lake rose over seven feet and triggered the first level of the emergency action plan as the lake level was two feet over the emergency spillway. This overflow from the reservoirs caused very high water levels down stream and produced minor to moderate flooding within the city of Ketchikan. The highest flow from the reservoirs coincided with high tide on December 14th and exacerbate the flooding situation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed on the Kobuk River near the village of Kobuk on the 29th and produced flooding on the 30th through the early morning of the 31st. A number of structures in the village were flooded. Damage amounts are estimated.
